challenges.title: Print a greeting
challenges.description: Print the text 'Hello, World!' to the console.
challenges.hint: Use console.log() with a string argument.

steps.title: Understand the task
steps.explanation: You need to display text in the console. JavaScript's console.log() writes any value to standard output.

steps.title: Write the solution
steps.explanation: Call console.log() with the string 'Hello, World!'.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Run the script. You should see 'Hello, World!' in the console.

challenges.title: Reverse a string
challenges.description: Write a function `reverseString(str)` that returns the input string reversed.
challenges.hint: Strings are immutable in JavaScript. Convert to an array, reverse it, then join it back.

steps.title: Split into characters
steps.explanation: str.split('') converts the string into an array of individual characters.

steps.title: Reverse the array
steps.explanation: Array.prototype.reverse() reverses the array in place and returns it.

steps.title: Join back to string
steps.explanation: Array.prototype.join('') concatenates all elements without a separator.

challenges.title: Debounce function
challenges.description: Implement a `debounce(fn, delay)` function that returns a debounced version of `fn`. The debounced function delays invoking `fn` until `delay` milliseconds have elapsed since the last invocation.
challenges.hint: Use a closure to hold a timer ID. Each call to the returned function clears the previous timer and starts a new one.

steps.title: Create a closure
steps.explanation: Declare a timerId variable in the outer scope so every call to the returned function shares the same timer reference.

steps.title: Clear and reset the timer
steps.explanation: On each invocation, clearTimeout cancels the pending call and setTimeout schedules a fresh one.
steps.code:
clearTimeout(timerId);
timerId = setTimeout(() => fn.apply(this, args), delay);

steps.title: Preserve context and arguments
steps.explanation: Use rest parameters (...args) and fn.apply(this, args) so the original function receives the correct this and all arguments.

challenges.title: Promise.all polyfill
challenges.description: Implement `promiseAll(promises)` that behaves like `Promise.all`: resolves with an array of results when all promises resolve, or rejects as soon as any promise rejects.
challenges.hint: Return a new Promise. Track a results array and a counter. Reject immediately on any rejection.

steps.title: Handle the empty case
steps.explanation: If the input array is empty, resolve immediately with an empty array.

steps.title: Track results by index
steps.explanation: Pre-allocate a results array of the same length so results are stored at the correct positions.

steps.title: Count resolutions
steps.explanation: Decrement a counter on each resolution. When it reaches zero, all promises have resolved — call resolve(results).
